subject: Chemistry
options: The limiting reagent is calcium metal.
solution:
Reaction: $Ca + 2HCl \rightarrow CaCl_2 + H_2$
Moles of Ca = $\frac{14.0}{40} = 0.35$ mol. Since HCl is in excess, Ca is limiting.
Option (1): $H_2$ evolved = 0.35 mol
Option (2): Volume of $H_2$ at STP = $0.35 \times 22.4 = 7.84$ L
Option (3): Ca is limiting reagent
Option (4): Mass of $CaCl_2 = 0.35 \times 111 = 38.85$ g, not 33.3 g
Statement (4) is incorrect.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The balanced redox equation is: $\text{MnO}_2 + 4\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MnCl}_2 + \text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
Molar mass of MnO₂ = 55 + 32 = 87 g/mol.
Moles of MnO₂ = $\frac{8.7}{87} = 0.1$ mol.
From stoichiometry, 1 mol MnO₂ produces 1 mol Cl₂, so 0.1 mol produces 0.1 mol Cl₂.
Mass of Cl₂ = $0.1 \times 71 = 7.1$ g.

solution:

& 120=\mathrm{M} \times 210 \\
& \mathrm{M}=\frac{120}{210}=0.57 \mathrm{~M}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
To determine the ion(s) present in very small quantity in the solution, we need to consider the chemical reaction that occurs when silver nitrate reacts with potassium iodide. The reaction between AgNO3 and KI is a double replacement reaction and can be represented as follows:

AgNO3 + KI → AgI + KNO3

Millimoles of AgNO3 = 25

Millimoles of KI = 25 × 1.05=26.25

∴KI is in excess & AgI forms negatively charged colloid. (Some Ag+remains in solution) lons Ag+& F-are therefore, present in very small quantity.

moles of N2stoichiometry of N2≠mole of H2stoichiometry of H2

molesstiochiometry ratio of N2=11=1
mole of H2=62=3

there is no limiting reagent.

mole of H2=42=2
molesstiochiometry ratio of H2=23=1.66

∴  Answer is 3 .

subject: Chemistry
Chemistry
solution:
$16.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{O}_3=\frac{16}{48}$ mole
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{16}{48} \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { molecules } \\
& =3 \times \frac{16}{48} \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } \\
& =6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } \\
& 28.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CO}=\frac{28}{28} \text { mole }=1 \text { mole } \\
& =1 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { molecules } \\
& =1 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } \\
& =6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } \\
& 16.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{O}_2=\frac{16}{16} \text { mole }=1 \mathrm{~mole}
\end{aligned}
$$
$=1 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}$ molecules
$=1 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}$ atoms
$=6.023 \times 10^{23}$ atoms
Therefore, the ratio is
$6.023 \times 10^{23}: 6.023 \times 10^{23}: 6.023 \times 10^{23}$
i.e. $1: 1: 1$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
KOH absorbs $CO_2$. Volume of $CO_2$ produced = $224 - 64 = 160$ mL
Unreacted $O_2 = 64$ mL, so $O_2$ consumed = $264 - 64 = 200$ mL
For hydrocarbon $C_xH_y$: $\frac{CO_2}{\text{Hydrocarbon}} = \frac{160}{80} = 2 \Rightarrow x = 2$
$O_2$ consumed per mole = $x + \frac{y}{4} = \frac{200}{80} = 2.5$
$2 + \frac{y}{4} = 2.5 \Rightarrow y = 2$
Hydrocarbon is $C_2H_2$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Volume of $SO_2$ at STP $= 1.4187$ L

Molar volume of an ideal gas at STP is approximately $22.414$ L.

Number of moles $n = \dfrac{\text{Volume}}{\text{Molar Volume}} = \dfrac{1.4187}{22.414} = 0.0633$

Number of molecules $= n \times N_A$

Number of molecules $= 0.0633 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 3.812 \times 10^{22}$

Answer: $0.0633; 3.812 \times 10^{22}$

solution:
The reaction is given as,

3PbCl2+2NH43PO4→Pb3PO42+6NH4Cl

The products formed in the above reaction is Pb3PO42 and NH4Cl  .

According to the above stoichiometric equation, three mole of each lead chloride and two moles of NH43PO4 is required to make one mole of Pb3PO42 and 6 moles of NH4Cl   

Here the Limiting Reagent is PbCl2

mmol of Pb3(PO4)2 formed

=mmol of PbCl2 reacted 3=723

=24 m mol

$75 \%$ by mass means :
$\because \quad 75 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{HNO}_3$ in 100 g solution.

$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{m}_{\text {solution }}=\frac{100 \times 30}{75} \mathrm{~g} \\

\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
For A: Molar mass of $H_2O = 18$ g/mol. Moles of $H_2O = \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{18} = 10^{-4}$. Since each $H_2O$ molecule has $3$ atoms, the number of atoms = $3 \times 10^{-4} \times N_A$.

For B: Molar mass of $H_2SO_4 = 98$ g/mol. Moles of $H_2SO_4 = \dfrac{9.8 \times 10^{-3}}{98} = 10^{-4}$. Since each $H_2SO_4$ molecule has $7$ atoms, the number of atoms = $7 \times 10^{-4} \times N_A$.

For C: Molar mass of $C = 12$ g/mol. Moles of $C = \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{12} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$. The number of atoms = $1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times N_A$.

For D: Molar mass of $NaCl = 58.5$ g/mol. Moles of $NaCl = \dfrac{5.85 \times 10^{-3}}{58.5} = 10^{-4}$. Since each $NaCl$ formula unit has $2$ atoms, the number of atoms = $2 \times 10^{-4} \times N_A$.

Thus, the correct matching is A-III, B-IV, C-II, D-I.

Answer: A-III, B-IV, C-II, D-I
